worth_it_explanation: Reporting accuracy is the bridge between what a publisher sees in the PubMatic console and what lands in the bank, so when the two disagree the publisher cannot tell whether the gap is a configuration mistake, a timing difference, or revenue that was earned but never paid. This check is a reconciliation of figures the publisher already has, the console reports and the remittance statements, against each other rather than a rebuild of the integration, so it costs reading and matching time. The finding points to a specific discrepancy the publisher can raise with PubMatic or correct in their own report settings.
intro: Reporting accuracy covers whether the revenue and fill figures a publisher reads in PubMatic reconcile with the money they are actually paid, and whether the reports are configured so the numbers can be compared across systems at all. A publisher monetizing through PubMatic sees one set of numbers in the console, another in their ad server, and a third on the remittance statement, and those three only agree when the time zones, currencies, and counting methods line up. These checks confirm that reported revenue and fill tie back to payments and that the report dimensions are set so the comparison is apples to apples rather than misaligned by a time-zone offset or a currency mismatch.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against actual payments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Console revenue and the money a publisher is paid can diverge for reasons that range from benign timing differences to revenue earned but never remitted. A publisher who never reconciles the two has no way to catch underpayment, a misconfigured payment account, or a reporting discrepancy that compounds month over month, so unreconciled reporting can hide a sustained revenue leak at the scale of the whole PubMatic integration.

educate.base:
PubMatic, like any sell-side platform (SSP), shows a publisher revenue and fill figures in its reporting console, and separately pays that publisher on a remittance schedule. The two are meant to describe the same earnings, but they are produced by different processes at different times, so they rarely match to the cent without a deliberate comparison. The console reports gross or net revenue for impressions as PubMatic counted them. The remittance reflects what was collected from buyers, after any deductions, for a billing period that may close on a different calendar than the report you are reading.

Several things can sit in the gap between the two figures. Some are expected, such as a revenue-share deduction, a payment threshold that holds small balances until they accumulate, or a net-payment term that pays a month or more after the revenue was earned. Others are not expected, such as revenue reported in the console that never appears on any remittance, a payment account or tax form that is misconfigured and holding funds, or a discrepancy between what PubMatic reported and what it actually collected. Without a reconciliation, a publisher cannot tell the benign reasons from the ones that cost money.

Reconciliation is the practice of matching console revenue for a defined period against the remittance that covers that same period, accounting for the known deductions and timing differences, and confirming that what remains lines up. When it does not, the leftover difference is the thing to investigate. A publisher who runs this monthly builds a record of what a normal gap looks like, which makes an abnormal one stand out.

A few signs point to revenue and fill that are not being reconciled:
  • No regular process matches console revenue for a period against the payment that covers that period
  • The publisher cannot explain the difference between a recent console figure and the matching remittance beyond the revenue share
  • Console revenue appears for a period that has no corresponding payment on record, and no one has asked why
  • Payment thresholds, net terms, or holds are unknown, so the publisher cannot separate a timing delay from a missing payment
You may want to reconcile PubMatic console revenue against the remittance statements for the same periods on a regular cadence, accounting for the revenue share and payment terms, and investigate any difference that the known deductions do not explain.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, revenue often swings with seasonal traffic and direct-sold campaigns that run alongside the programmatic inventory, so a month-to-month change in console revenue is not by itself a red flag. You may want to reconcile each period against its own remittance rather than comparing raw totals across periods, so a seasonal dip is not mistaken for an underpayment and an actual shortfall is not lost in the noise of normal variation.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, programmatic revenue is usually a smaller line next to product margin, which can make an SSP payment discrepancy easy to overlook. You may want to fold the PubMatic reconciliation into the same monthly close that handles the rest of the site's revenue, so the ad income is checked against payment with the same rigor as any other receivable rather than accepted on the console figure alone.

learn_more.source_title: sellers.json and SupplyChain Object Supply Chain Transparency

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed reporting and payment documentation, including how console revenue maps to remittances and what payment terms apply, is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Pull console revenue for a defined period and the remittance that covers the same period, then match them while accounting for the revenue share, payment threshold, and net terms.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Open the reporting section and build a revenue report for a closed period, such as a full calendar month, with total revenue, paid impressions, and fill rate.
Note whether the figure shown is gross or net of the revenue share since that determines what you compare it against.
Record the period boundaries and the time zone the report uses, so the period matches the remittance period rather than overlapping it.

remittance_statements.label: Remittance and payment statements
steps: Gather the PubMatic payment statement or remittance that covers the same period as the console report.
Identify the revenue share, any payment threshold holding a balance, and the net-payment term, so you know the expected difference between earned and paid.
Match the console revenue against the payment after applying those known deductions, and record any difference that remains unexplained, along with the periods it covers.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records console revenue for a defined period, the remittance that covers the same period, and the known deductions of revenue share, payment threshold, and net terms. A reconciliation where the console revenue ties to the payment once the known deductions are applied, with any residual difference explained, passes. A setup with no reconciliation process, or one where a residual difference has no explanation, or where reported revenue has no corresponding payment, is a fail recorded with the periods and amounts that do not reconcile.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the console revenue, the matching remittance, and the known deductions above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not reconcile the revenue and fill reported in its PubMatic console against the payments actually received. Console figures and remittances are produced by separate processes on different schedules, so a deduction such as the revenue share, a payment threshold, or net terms can explain part of the gap, but without a reconciliation a missing payment or a reporting discrepancy looks the same as a normal timing delay. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling console revenue against the remittance for each matching period on a regular cadence, applying the known deductions, and investigating any difference that those deductions do not account for. Once a normal gap is established, an abnormal one becomes visible and can be raised with PubMatic before it compounds.

audit_checks.title: Report time zone and currency are not aligned for cross-system comparison
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the PubMatic console, the ad server, and the remittance each count in a different time zone or report in a different currency, the same day's revenue lands in different buckets and the totals will not match even when nothing is wrong. A publisher comparing misaligned reports can chase a phantom discrepancy or, worse, dismiss a real one as a known time-zone quirk, so misaligned dimensions undermine every comparison the publisher tries to make.

educate.base:
A revenue number means nothing on its own. It means something only against a period, a time zone, and a currency, and a publisher monetizing through PubMatic reads numbers from at least three places that may not agree on any of the three. The PubMatic console reports in the time zone and currency set on the account. The ad server, often Google Ad Manager, reports in its own network time zone and currency. The remittance closes on a billing calendar that may differ again. When these are not aligned, the same impressions fall into different days and the same dollars are expressed in different units, so the totals diverge for reasons that have nothing to do with an actual problem.

Time zone is the most common source of a false discrepancy. If the console reports on a Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) day and the ad server reports on a Pacific day, the boundary between any two days sits several hours apart, so revenue near midnight shifts from one day to the next between the two systems. Compare a single day across them and the numbers will not match, not because either is wrong but because they are counting different windows. The same logic applies at the month boundary, where a few hours of misalignment moves revenue between billing periods.

Currency creates a quieter version of the same problem. When bidders pay in several currencies and the systems convert at different rates or on different days, the converted totals drift apart. In a header-bidding setup, the wrapper applies its own currency conversion before sending bids to the ad server, so a publisher running an OpenWrap or other Prebid-based wrapper needs the wrapper's currency configuration, the SSP's reporting currency, and the ad server's currency to describe the same money the same way. Prebid's currency module documents how that conversion is configured on the wrapper side.

The fix is alignment before comparison. A publisher confirms that the reports being compared use the same time zone and the same currency, or applies a known, consistent offset when they cannot be made identical, before reading any cross-system number as a match or a mismatch. A few signs point to dimensions that are not aligned:
  • The PubMatic console and the ad server report in different time zones, and reports are compared day to day without adjusting for the offset
  • Bidder revenue arrives in multiple currencies and the systems convert at different rates or on different days
  • A recurring discrepancy near day or month boundaries is dismissed as a known quirk rather than traced to a time-zone difference
  • The currency settings on the header-bidding wrapper, the SSP, and the ad server have never been checked against each other
You may want to confirm that the time zone and currency are aligned across the PubMatic console, the ad server, and the wrapper before comparing their figures, so a discrepancy you find reflects a real difference rather than a counting mismatch.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site with an international audience, impressions arrive around the clock, so a time-zone offset between the console and the ad server spreads revenue across the day boundary in both directions and inflates the apparent discrepancy. You may want to set both systems to the same reporting time zone, so a day in one is the same day in the other and the comparison is clean.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, programmatic revenue may be consolidated into financial reporting that runs in the business's local currency. You may want to confirm the currency PubMatic reports in matches the currency the ad server and the finance team use, so the ad revenue is not converted twice or compared in mixed units when it reaches the books.

learn_more.source_title: Analytics for Prebid.js for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's account-level reporting settings, including the console time zone and reporting currency, are configured in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Check the time zone and currency on the PubMatic console, the ad server, and the header-bidding wrapper, then confirm they match or that a consistent offset is applied before any comparison.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Open the account or reporting settings and record the time zone the console reports in and the currency it reports in.
Build a revenue report for a single day and note the day boundary the report uses, so it can be lined up against the ad server's day.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Open the ad server, often Google Ad Manager, and record its network time zone and reporting currency.
Compare those to the PubMatic settings and note any difference in time zone or currency.
Pull the same single day from the ad server and the console and check whether the revenue lines up or shifts across the day boundary, which points to a time-zone offset.

wrapper_config.label: Header-bidding wrapper configuration
steps: If the publisher runs an OpenWrap or other Prebid-based wrapper, review the currency module configuration and record the ad-server currency it converts bids into.
Confirm that the wrapper currency, the SSP reporting currency, and the ad-server currency describe the same money in the same units, and note any mismatch.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the time zone and currency for the PubMatic console, the ad server, and the header-bidding wrapper, and the result of comparing a single day across them. Reports that share a time zone and currency, or that apply a known consistent offset and convert at a consistent rate, pass. Reports compared across systems with a different time zone or currency and no adjustment are a fail recorded with the systems and dimensions that do not align.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the time zone and currency for the console, the ad server, and the wrapper above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} compares revenue across the PubMatic console, the ad server, and the header-bidding wrapper without aligning their time zones and currencies first. When one system counts a Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) day and another counts a local day, the same impressions fall into different days, and when bidders pay in several currencies converted at different rates, the totals drift, so the figures diverge for reasons unrelated to any real problem. {{voice_recommend}} setting the console, the ad server, and the wrapper to the same time zone and reporting currency, or applying a known consistent offset where they cannot match, before reading any cross-system figure. With the dimensions aligned, a remaining discrepancy reflects a real difference worth investigating rather than a counting mismatch.
